note: Health Check indicator-level success criteria. 5 dimensions × 5 indicators × 5 levels. Level 1 = Urgent, Level 5 = Confident. Unobservable indicators on a given visit should be left unscored — dimension average only counts rated indicators.

accessibilityByDesign.label: Accessibility by Design
accessibilityByDesign.observability: Mostly observable via resources shared in Teams, on screen, or in lesson materials.

indicators.label: Uses Accessibility Checker
indicators.observability: Observable — check if files in Teams have been run through Checker (green tick in Word/PowerPoint). Ask to demonstrate.

levels.1: No awareness of the Accessibility Checker. Has never used it. Resources shared without any checking.
levels.2: Aware the tool exists but has never used it or used it only once. No habit formed.
levels.3: Uses the Accessibility Checker occasionally but not as a routine before sharing. Some resources pass; others don't.
levels.4: Runs Accessibility Checker before sharing most resources. Minor gaps remain (e.g. older files not updated).
levels.5: Accessibility Checker is automatic — every resource checked before sharing. Issues fixed before distribution. Could demonstrate to a peer.

indicators.label: Alt text on images
indicators.observability: Observable — inspect images in shared PowerPoints or Word docs. Right-click → Edit Alt Text.

levels.1: No alt text on any images. No awareness that alt text is needed or why.
levels.2: Knows alt text exists but rarely applies it. Most images have no description.
levels.3: Adds alt text to some images — typically those just created, not retrospectively. Quality varies.
levels.4: Alt text present on most images. Some older resources not updated. Descriptions are functional if basic.
levels.5: All images have meaningful, descriptive alt text. Marks decorative images as decorative. Can explain why to learners.

indicators.label: Colour contrast checked
indicators.observability: Observable — inspect slide decks and documents. Look for light-on-light or dark-on-dark combinations.

levels.1: No awareness of colour contrast as an accessibility issue. Uses colours chosen purely for aesthetics.
levels.2: Aware contrast matters but applies no systematic check. May use problematic combinations unknowingly.
levels.3: Takes some care with colour choices but doesn't verify against WCAG 2.2 AA standards. Some slides/docs still fail.
levels.4: Consciously checks that text is readable against backgrounds. Uses high-contrast defaults in most materials.
levels.5: All materials meet WCAG 2.2 AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text). Uses contrast-checking tools. Never relies on colour alone to convey meaning.

indicators.label: Captions/transcripts on video
indicators.observability: Observable — play any video shared in Teams or embedded in resources. Check captions are enabled/provided.

levels.1: Videos shared with no captions or transcripts. No awareness this is required.
levels.2: Aware captions should exist but relies on auto-captions only, without reviewing accuracy.
levels.3: Enables auto-captions on videos. Does not create or edit transcripts. Some videos still shared without.
levels.4: Auto-captions enabled consistently. Reviews them for accuracy on key videos. Transcripts provided for important content.
levels.5: All video content captioned. Captions reviewed and corrected where needed. Transcripts provided. Learners explicitly told how to use them.

indicators.label: Accessible assignment briefs
indicators.observability: Observable — inspect assignment briefs in Teams Assignments or shared folders. Check headings, font, structure.

levels.1: Briefs shared as images, scanned PDFs, or dense unformatted text. No headings, no structure. Inaccessible by design.
levels.2: Briefs are typed but formatting is ad-hoc. No consistent heading structure. Font choice or size may cause issues.
levels.3: Some formatting applied (bold headings, readable font) but not systematic. Not checked with Accessibility Checker.
levels.4: Briefs use clear headings, readable font (12pt+), good spacing. Passes Accessibility Checker. Minor improvements possible.
levels.5: Briefs are fully accessible: proper heading hierarchy, readable font and size, sufficient spacing, passes checker, available in alternative formats on request.

promotingAccessiblePractice.label: Promoting Accessible Practice
promotingAccessiblePractice.observability: Mostly observable during lesson delivery or from learner testimony. Some aspects require direct observation.

indicators.label: Proactively offers Immersive Reader
indicators.observability: Observable — watch whether teacher mentions Immersive Reader during lesson or in Teams resources. Can also ask learners.

levels.1: Has never mentioned Immersive Reader to learners. Unaware it exists or how to activate it.
levels.2: Aware of Immersive Reader but has not demonstrated it to learners. May mention it if directly asked.
levels.3: Has shown Immersive Reader to learners at least once but does not offer it routinely. Relies on learners to self-identify need.
levels.4: Mentions Immersive Reader regularly. Demonstrates it at start of term. Most learners know it exists.
levels.5: Proactively offers Immersive Reader to all learners as a standard tool. Demonstrates it in every new topic or resource. Learners use it independently.

indicators.label: Mentions AT tools in lessons
indicators.observability: Observable — watch for verbal or visual references to Read&Write, Live Captions, Dictate, Focus Mode during lesson.

levels.1: No mention of any AT tools in lesson. Assistive technology is invisible in teaching practice.
levels.2: AT tools mentioned only when a specific learner flags a need. No proactive normalisation.
levels.3: Mentions one or two AT tools occasionally. Not embedded into lesson structure or delivery.
levels.4: Refers to AT tools (Immersive Reader, Live Captions, Read&Write) in most lessons. Knows the key tools available.
levels.5: AT tools woven into every lesson naturally. Mentions Read&Write, Dictate, Live Captions, Focus Mode as standard options. Learners reminded regularly.

indicators.label: Accessibility offered universally — not SEND-only
indicators.observability: Observable — does the teacher offer tools to all learners or only direct SEND learners to them? Ask learners.

levels.1: Accessibility tools only discussed with learners who have a formal SEND diagnosis. Other learners never offered them.
levels.2: Mainly directed at SEND learners. Occasionally mentions to others but not as a universal offer.
levels.3: Becoming more universal but still defaults to SEND-focused framing (e.g. 'if you have dyslexia...').
levels.4: Frames accessibility tools as useful for everyone. Offers them broadly. Occasional slip back to SEND-only language.
levels.5: Explicitly positions accessibility as universal ('these tools help everyone'). No SEND-only framing. Normalised for whole class.

indicators.label: Learners personalise their digital environment
indicators.observability: Observable — can learners be seen adjusting display settings, using preferred tools, or navigating independently?

levels.1: Learners have no awareness that their digital environment can be personalised. No personalisation visible.
levels.2: Some learners may have set preferences but this has not been taught or encouraged by the teacher.
levels.3: Teacher has mentioned personalisation options once or twice. A few learners use them but most don't know how.
levels.4: Most learners aware of basic personalisation (e.g. dark mode, text size, Immersive Reader settings). Some use them.
levels.5: Learners confidently personalise their environment. Teacher has taught them how. Diverse personalisation visible across the class.

indicators.label: AT embedded into routine delivery
indicators.observability: Observable — is AT referenced in lesson plans, slide decks, assignment briefs, Teams resources, or verbal delivery?

levels.1: AT is entirely absent from lesson design and delivery. Not referenced anywhere.
levels.2: AT appears occasionally but only as a reactive response to a learner difficulty, not planned.
levels.3: AT is planned for some lessons but not consistently. Referenced in some resources but not built into delivery.
levels.4: AT is part of most lesson plans. Teacher consistently references it. Embedded in Teams resources and delivery.
levels.5: AT is fully embedded: referenced in lesson plans, Teams channels, assignment briefs, and verbal delivery. Learners expect it as standard.

inclusiveKnowledgeAndPractice.label: Inclusive Knowledge & Practice
inclusiveKnowledgeAndPractice.observability: Mix of observable (lesson design, resource variety) and inference from conversation and learner outcomes.

indicators.label: Aware of SEND needs and adjusts digitally
indicators.observability: Partially observable — look for differentiated digital resources, personalised Teams setups, or ask about specific adjustments made.

levels.1: No awareness of how SEND needs connect to digital practice. Adjustments not considered in resource design.
levels.2: Aware learners have needs but digital adjustments are not made. Refers learners to SEND team without digital follow-through.
levels.3: Makes some digital adjustments for known SEND learners (e.g. larger font, different colour background) but not systematically.
levels.4: Proactively considers SEND needs when designing digital resources. Most learners with identified needs have adjustments in place.
levels.5: Fully informed about cohort SEND needs. Digital adjustments made proactively for all. Reviews and updates as needs change. Works with SEND team.

indicators.label: Applies UDL — multiple means of engagement
indicators.observability: Observable — look for variety in how content is presented (text, audio, video, visual) and how learners can respond.

levels.1: Single delivery format throughout. All learners receive the same resource in the same way. No variation.
levels.2: Occasionally provides a second format (e.g. a video alongside text) but not by design — ad hoc.
levels.3: Some UDL principles applied — at least two means of representation visible. Not yet consistent across lessons.
levels.4: Multiple means of representation used consistently. Learners offered some choice in how they engage with content.
levels.5: UDL embedded across all lessons. Multiple means of representation, expression, and engagement. Learner choice visible and genuinely offered.

indicators.label: Provides digital alternatives or choices
indicators.observability: Observable — do learners have a genuine choice of how to submit or engage? Are alternatives offered in assignment briefs?

levels.1: One submission format, one way to engage. No alternatives offered digitally.
levels.2: Occasionally allows a different format if a learner requests it. Not built into the design.
levels.3: Some tasks offer a digital alternative (e.g. typed or spoken response). Not consistent across all tasks.
levels.4: Most tasks offer at least two ways to engage or submit. Alternatives are signposted clearly.
levels.5: Digital alternatives are designed in from the start. Learners routinely choose how to engage and submit. Choices are genuinely equivalent.

indicators.label: Resources designed for equitable access
indicators.observability: Observable — inspect resources. Are font sizes readable, layouts clean, images explained, language plain and clear?

levels.1: Resources designed for a typical learner only. Dense text, small font, no visual support. Significant barriers for some.
levels.2: Some basic readability considered (font not tiny, headings present) but not systematically designed for access.
levels.3: Resources are broadly readable. Some accessibility features present. Not checked against any standard.
levels.4: Resources consistently readable: appropriate font, spacing, plain language, supported with visuals. Passes basic accessibility checks.
levels.5: Resources explicitly designed for equitable access. Tested against WCAG 2.2 AA. Plain language used. Visual hierarchy clear. Reviewed with learner feedback.

indicators.label: Learner voice used to adapt digital practice
indicators.observability: Partially observable — ask if teacher seeks feedback on digital experience. Look for evidence of practice changing in response.

levels.1: No learner feedback sought on digital practice. No awareness that learners can inform this.
levels.2: Learner feedback collected generally but digital practice never specifically asked about or adapted.
levels.3: Has asked learners about digital tools or resources at least once. Some changes made informally.
levels.4: Regularly seeks feedback on digital experience. Some changes to practice evidenced as a result.
levels.5: Learner voice systematically informs digital practice. Feedback sought, recorded, and acted on. Learners see their input reflected in how teaching evolves.

filesAndFoldersPermissions.label: Files, Folders & Permissions
filesAndFoldersPermissions.observability: Directly observable — inspect Teams channels, SharePoint folders, and shared files. Can be done during or after lesson.

indicators.label: Consistent file naming conventions
indicators.observability: Observable — look at files in Teams Assignments, shared folders, or OneDrive. Are names consistent and meaningful?

levels.1: Files named randomly (e.g. 'Document1', 'final FINAL v3', dates in inconsistent formats). No convention.
levels.2: Some descriptive naming but inconsistent. Mix of conventions or no convention at all. Hard to find files.
levels.3: Most files have descriptive names but convention is informal. No agreed standard. Occasional confusing names.
levels.4: Files consistently named with clear descriptors and dates or versions. Easy to navigate. Minor inconsistencies.
levels.5: Consistent naming convention applied to all files: descriptive, dated, versioned where needed. Could explain convention to a new colleague.

indicators.label: SharePoint permissions set appropriately
indicators.observability: Observable — check sharing settings on key files/folders. Look for 'Anyone with the link' vs targeted sharing.

levels.1: Files shared with 'Anyone with the link' including sensitive or confidential content. No awareness of risk.
levels.2: Uses broad sharing links without considering appropriateness. Not aware of the difference between sharing options.
levels.3: Beginning to use more targeted sharing but inconsistent. Some files still over-shared. Not always sure how to check.
levels.4: Most files shared appropriately (organisation members, specific people). Occasional over-sharing. Knows how to check and adjust.
levels.5: SharePoint permissions reviewed and set correctly on all shared content. Sensitive files restricted. Could teach others to do the same.

indicators.label: Folders structured and clearly labelled
indicators.observability: Observable — ask to see Teams channels or SharePoint folders. Look at hierarchy, naming, and findability.

levels.1: No folder structure. Files dumped in one location. Impossible to navigate without knowing exactly what you're looking for.
levels.2: Some folders exist but named vaguely or inconsistently. Structure not logical. Learners struggle to find resources.
levels.3: Basic folder structure in place. Main resources findable. Subfolders inconsistent or missing. Needs tidying.
levels.4: Clear folder structure: module/topic/week organised logically. Labels descriptive. Learners can navigate independently.
levels.5: Well-structured folder hierarchy that mirrors the curriculum. Named consistently. Reviewed regularly. Learners and colleagues can find anything quickly.

indicators.label: GDPR-compliant sharing
indicators.observability: Partially observable — look for personal data (learner names, grades, health info) in shared or open files.

levels.1: Personal learner data (names, grades, health/SEND info) shared in open files or links. Significant GDPR risk.
levels.2: Some awareness of GDPR but personal data occasionally included in shared resources without appropriate controls.
levels.3: Generally avoids putting personal data in shared resources but not always consistent. Unsure about edge cases.
levels.4: Personal data handled appropriately. Not included in open-access resources. Shares only aggregated or anonymised data.
levels.5: Fully GDPR-compliant digital practice. Personal data kept in secure, access-controlled locations only. Could advise colleagues on what is and isn't acceptable.

indicators.label: Version control — no 'final_v3' files
indicators.observability: Observable — check file names in Teams and shared folders for evidence of version sprawl.

levels.1: Multiple versions of the same file with confusing names (final, final2, USE THIS ONE). No clear current version.
levels.2: Some version confusion present but not severe. Occasionally can't tell which version is current.
levels.3: Mostly maintains one working version but occasionally saves duplicates. No formal version control.
levels.4: Consistent use of versioning in file names (e.g. v1, v2) or relies on OneDrive version history. Current version clear.
levels.5: Uses OneDrive/SharePoint version history or clear naming conventions. No redundant files. Archives old versions in a separate folder. Could teach this to others.

effectiveCommunication.label: Effective Digital Communication
effectiveCommunication.observability: Observable — review Teams channels, posts, announcements, and assignment feedback visible during visit.

indicators.label: Teams channel/class space is organised
indicators.observability: Observable — open Teams and view the class space. Look at channel structure, pinned resources, and General channel posts.

levels.1: Teams space either doesn't exist, is entirely empty, or is so cluttered it's unusable. Learners have no functional digital class space.
levels.2: Teams space exists but minimal setup. Resources missing, channels not organised, learners unlikely to use it.
levels.3: Teams space is functional. Basic resources present but not curated. Some clutter. Navigation possible but effortful.
levels.4: Well-organised Teams space. Relevant resources pinned or in Files. Posts are purposeful. Learners directed there regularly.
levels.5: Teams space is a curated digital environment. Clear channels, pinned resources up to date, organised Files tab, consistent communication. Learners use it as their primary learning hub.

indicators.label: Posts and messages are clear and professional
indicators.observability: Observable — read recent posts in Teams General channel and any announcements. Check tone, clarity, and professionalism.

levels.1: No posts or messages. Or posts are unclear, informal to the point of confusion, or contain errors that undermine confidence.
levels.2: Posts exist but are irregular and sometimes unclear. Tone may be too informal or messages lack key information.
levels.3: Posts are generally professional and clear. Occasional inconsistency in tone or detail. Learners mostly understand what is expected.
levels.4: Posts and messages consistently professional, clear, and timely. Learners know what they need to do. Rare confusion.
levels.5: Exemplary digital communication. Clear, professional, warm tone. Posts anticipate learner questions. Messages include everything learners need. Could be used as a model.

indicators.label: Appropriate Teams features used
indicators.observability: Observable — look for use of Assignments (not just General posts), announcements, tags, tabs, and relevant channels.

levels.1: Only uses General channel. No Assignments, no tabs, no structured features. Teams used like a basic messaging app.
levels.2: Uses Assignments occasionally but not consistently. Other features (tabs, announcements, channels) largely unused.
levels.3: Uses Assignments regularly. Some additional features used (e.g. a Resources tab). Not yet using the full potential.
levels.4: Uses Assignments, appropriate channels, and tabs effectively. Announcements used for key information. Good feature awareness.
levels.5: Full and purposeful use of Teams features: Assignments with rubrics, relevant channels, tabs, tags, announcements. Each feature used for a clear pedagogical reason.

indicators.label: Resources proactively shared
indicators.observability: Observable — check Teams Files tab and recent posts. Are resources uploaded before or alongside lessons, not after?

levels.1: Resources not shared digitally. Learners have no way to access materials outside the lesson.
levels.2: Resources occasionally uploaded to Teams but inconsistently and often after the lesson.
levels.3: Most lesson resources shared on Teams. Some gaps. Learners mostly know where to find them.
levels.4: Resources shared in advance of or alongside lessons consistently. Learners can prepare and revisit independently.
levels.5: Resources proactively shared with clear organisation and signposting. Learners directed to specific materials. Includes supplementary resources for further learning.

indicators.label: Communication is accessible and inclusive
indicators.observability: Observable — check if posts use plain language, include alt text on images, and are formatted for readability.

levels.1: Communication assumes all learners can read and engage with standard text without support. No accessibility consideration.
levels.2: Accessible communication considered occasionally but not consistently. Most posts are plain text with no formatting support.
levels.3: Some accessible formatting used in posts (bold key info, short paragraphs). Not systematic. No alt text on images in posts.
levels.4: Posts formatted for readability. Plain language used. Key information highlighted. Some consideration for learners with English as additional language.
levels.5: All communication is accessible: plain language, clear formatting, alt text on images in posts, inclusive of ESOL and SEND learners. Tone is warm and welcoming for all.
